title: Longest Consecutive Streak
description:
Given an unsorted array of integers, return the length of the longest sequence of consecutive values. The algorithm should run in O(n) time. Duplicate values do not extend the streak and should be ignored.

    

Constraints:
    - 0 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10^5
- -10^9 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^9
explanation:
Intuition
A value can be the start of a streak only if its predecessor is absent. By storing all numbers in a set, we can check starts in O(1), then walk forward while next numbers exist. Each element participates in at most one forward scan, yielding linear time.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Insert all values into a set.
2. Main Algorithm: For each value x, if x−1 is not in the set, expand a run x, x+1, x+2,... while present and track the length.
3. Return Result: Return the maximum streak length found.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the algorithm scans elements a constant
  number of times and performs O(1) work for each update or lookup.
- Space Complexity: O(n) due to the auxiliary structures required to
  store counts, prefix sums, or membership sets while ensuring fast access.

Key Insights
- Checking only starts avoids repeated scans over the same run
- Sets provide O(1) membership tests crucial for linear time

Common Pitfalls
- Scanning forward from every element causes quadratic time
- Forgetting to ignore duplicates can inflate counts

testCases.explanation: Duplicates do not extend the run

hints: How do you detect the start of a sequence in O(1)
Try a set to test presence of predecessors and successors
Only expand when a predecessor is missing
